blocks.text: Psalm 27
Vindicate me, O Lord, for I have walked in my integrity, and I have trusted in the Lord without wavering.

Ps 26: A declaration of righteousness and a plea for salvation and a declaration of righteousness. An individual petition of someone who feels unworthy, possibly a priest. “Walking” occurs at the psalm’s beginning and end, suggesting participation in sacred processions.
1 : The priest asks God to his claims of right conduct.
Prove me, O Lord, and try me; test my heart and mind.
For your steadfast love is before my eyes, and I walk in faithfulness to you.
I do not sit with the worthless, nor do I consort with hypocrites;
4 : implies partnering (1 Sam 2.8; Ps 101.6).
I hate the company of evildoers, and will not sit with the wicked.
4–5 : As in Pss 1 and 101, the worshiper (rejects) the company and the projects of the wicked.
I wash my hands in innocence, and go around your altar, O Lord,
6 : Washing is commanded in priestly rituals (Ex 30.21).
singing aloud a song of thanksgiving, and telling all your wondrous deeds.
O Lord, I love the house in which you dwell, and the place where your glory abides.
Do not sweep me away with sinners, nor my life with the bloodthirsty,
those in whose hands are evil devices, and whose right hands are full of bribes.
8–10 : In contrast to “hating” the wicked in vv. 4–5, the psalmist “loves,” in the sense of choosing, God’s house and ways.
But as for me, I walk in my integrity; redeem me, and be gracious to me.
11 : Confident of God’s acceptance, the psalmist resolves to continue walking the right path.
My foot stands on level ground; in the great congregation I will bless the Lord.
